subject: Physics
q: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. The time taken, for disintegrating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> part of its original mass will be:
question: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. The time taken, for disintegrating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> part of its original mass will be:
solution:
It is given that t12= T.

After disintegration of 78thpart, 18th part is only left.

Using the decay formula,

N=N012tt12

⇒N08=N012tT⇒-ln8=-tTln2⇒3ln2=tTln2⇒t=3T

subject: Physics
q: A freshly prepared radioactive source of half life <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> hours <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> minutes emits radiation which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> times the permissible safe level. The minimum time, after which it would be possible to work safely with source, will be _____ hours.
question: A freshly prepared radioactive source of half life <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> hours <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> minutes emits radiation which is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>64</mn></math> times the permissible safe level. The minimum time, after which it would be possible to work safely with source, will be _____ hours.
solution:
Given here, T=150 minutes,

A0=64x, where, x is the safe limit.

Using radioactive decay law relation, A=A0×2-tT, where, t is the minimum time in hours after which it would be possible to work safely with source.

Putting the values, we have 

x=64x×2-tT

∴  t=6 T=6×2.5=15 hours

subject: Physics
q:
In a hypothetical fission reaction
${ }_{92} X^{236} \rightarrow{ }_{56} Y^{141}+{ }_{36} Z^{92}+3 R$
The identity of emitted particles ( $\mathrm{R})$ is :
question:
In a hypothetical fission reaction
${ }_{92} X^{236} \rightarrow{ }_{56} Y^{141}+{ }_{36} Z^{92}+3 R$
The identity of emitted particles ( $\mathrm{R})$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Z} \text { in LHS }=92 \\ & \mathrm{Z} \text { in } \mathrm{RHS}=56+36=92 \\ & \mathrm{~A} \text { in } \mathrm{LHS}=236 \\ & \mathrm{~A} \text { in } \mathrm{RHS}=141+92=233 \end{aligned}$
So 3 neutrons are released.

q: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Tm=30 ms
C=200 μF
qN=Q0e-tRCN0e-λ,t=Q0 N0eλ-1RC

Since qN is constant hence
λ=1RC
R=1λC=TmC=30×10-3200×10-6=150 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Half lives of two radioactive nuclei <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are 10 minutes and 20 minutes, respectively. If, initially a sample has equal number of nuclei, then after 60 minutes, the ratio of decayed numbers of nuclei A and B will be:
question: Half lives of two radioactive nuclei <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> are 10 minutes and 20 minutes, respectively. If, initially a sample has equal number of nuclei, then after 60 minutes, the ratio of decayed numbers of nuclei A and B will be:
solution:
Half-life of A : t12(A)=10  minutes
Number of half-lives in 60 minutes are: 6010=6
∴  Number of decayed nuclei of A
NA=No-No26=6364No
Similarly, Half-life of B : t12(B)=20  minutes
Number of half-lives of B in 60 minutes are: 6020=3
∴ Number of decayed nuclei of B
NB=No-No23=78No
∴  NA:NB=6364:78=9:8=9:8

question: Two radioactive materials <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> , respectively. solution:
NA=N0e-10λt
NB=N0e-λt

1=9λt

The activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup></math> curie after

solution:
Given here, A0=6.4×10-4 Curie and A=5×10−6 Curie

Half life, T12=5 days 

The activity of a radioactive sample at time t is given by,

A=A012tT12

⇒5×10-66.4×10-4=12t5

⇒7=t5⇒t=35 days

subject: Physics
q: A solution containing active cobalt ${ }_{27}^{60} \mathrm{Co}$ having activity of $0.8 \mu \mathrm{Ci}$ and decay constant $\lambda$ is injected in an animal's body. If $1 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of blood is drawn from the animal's body after $10 \mathrm{hrs}$ of injection, the activity found was 300 decays per minute. What is the volume of blood that is flowing in the body? $\left(1 \mathrm{Ci}=3.7 \times 10^{10}\right.$ decay per second and at $\left.\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{hrs}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}=0.84\right)$
question: A solution containing active cobalt ${ }_{27}^{60} \mathrm{Co}$ having activity of $0.8 \mu \mathrm{Ci}$ and decay constant $\lambda$ is injected in an animal's body. If $1 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of blood is drawn from the animal's body after $10 \mathrm{hrs}$ of injection, the activity found was 300 decays per minute. What is the volume of blood that is flowing in the body? $\left(1 \mathrm{Ci}=3.7 \times 10^{10}\right.$ decay per second and at $\left.\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{hrs}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}=0.84\right)$
solution:
Let initial activity $=\mathrm{No}=0.8 \mu \mathrm{ci}$ $0.8 \times 3.7 \times 10^4 \mathrm{dps}$
Activity in $1 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of blood at $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{hr}$, $\mathrm{n}=\frac{300}{60} \mathrm{dps}=5 \mathrm{dps}$
$\mathrm{N}=$ Activity of whole blood at time $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{hr}$. Total volume of the blood in the person, V $=\frac{N}{n}$
$=\frac{N_0 e-\lambda t}{n}=\frac{0.8 \times 3.7 \times 10^4 \times 0.7927}{5} \cong 5$ litre

q: A nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Another nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If relation between mass number is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo></math> _______.
question: A nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Another nucleus has mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. If relation between mass number is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msub><mi>A</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfrac><mo>=</mo></math> _______.
solution:
For a nucleus we can write volume: V=43πR3

Now, R=R0A13

Therefore, V=43πR03A

⇒V2V1=A2A1=4

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of the density of oxygen nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mprescripts/><mn>8</mn><mn>16</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math> and helium nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts/><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math> is
question: The ratio of the density of oxygen nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mprescripts/><mn>8</mn><mn>16</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math> and helium nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi>He</mi><mprescripts/><mn>2</mn><mn>4</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math> is
solution:
The radius of a nucleus, R is related to its atomic number A as:

R=R0A13

where R0 is the nuclear radius of hydrogen.

Thus, the density of a nucleus is given by:

d=μA43πR0A133=3μ4πR03=Constant 

(Here, μ is atomic mass unit)

Hence, the ratio of nuclear density of oxygen to helium nucleus is 1:1

question: A radioactive sample decays <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> times its original quantity in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn></math> minutes. The half-life of the sample is
solution:
Remaining amount of sample is=1-78=18

Using radioactive decay law

N=N02-tT12

We have, 2tT12=N0N=N0N08=8=23

Then, tT12=3

Thus, half life of the sample is T12=5 min.

subject: Physics
q: A nucleus has mass number $\alpha$ and radius $R_{\alpha}$. Another nucleus has mass number $\beta$ and radius $R_{\beta}$. If $\beta=8 \alpha$ then $R_{\alpha} / R_{\beta}$ is:
question: A nucleus has mass number $\alpha$ and radius $R_{\alpha}$. Another nucleus has mass number $\beta$ and radius $R_{\beta}$. If $\beta=8 \alpha$ then $R_{\alpha} / R_{\beta}$ is:
solution:
Nuclear radius follows $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$ where $A$ is mass number.
Given $\beta = 8\alpha$, we have $R_\alpha = R_0 \alpha^{1/3}$ and $R_\beta = R_0 (8\alpha)^{1/3} = R_0 \cdot 2 \alpha^{1/3} = 2R_\alpha$.
Thus $R_\alpha/R_\beta = 1/2 = 0.5$.

q:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
question:
In the following nuclear reaction,
D→αD1→β-D2→αD3→γD4
Mass number of D is 182 and atomic number is 74 . Mass number and atomic number of D4 respectively will be
solution:
Equivalent reaction can be written as D→D4+2α+β-+γ

So, mass number of D4=mass number of D-2×4=182-8=174

Atomic number of D4=atomic number of D-2×2+1=74-4+1=71

The reaction is 

D74182⟶αD172178⟶β-D273178⟶αD371174→γD471174

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following pair of nuclei are isobars of the element?
question: Which of the following pair of nuclei are isobars of the element?
solution:
Isobars are atoms of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons (mass number $A$), but different atomic numbers ($Z$).
In the given options:
(1) $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$: Both have the same mass number $A = 3$. Since they have different atomic numbers ($Z=1$ and $Z=2$), they are isobars.
(2) $_1^2\text{H}$ and $_1^3\text{H}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=1$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
(3) $_{80}^{198}\text{Hg}$ and $_{79}^{197}\text{Au}$: These have different mass numbers ($198$ and $197$).
(4) $_{92}^{236}\text{U}$ and $_{92}^{238}\text{U}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=92$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
Thus, the pair $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$ are isobars.

subject: Physics
q: An atom ${ }_{3}^{8} X$ is bombarded by shower of fundamental particles and in 10 s this atom absorbed 10 electrons, 10 protons and 9 neutrons. The percentage growth in the surface area of the nucleons is recorded by:
question: An atom ${ }_{3}^{8} X$ is bombarded by shower of fundamental particles and in 10 s this atom absorbed 10 electrons, 10 protons and 9 neutrons. The percentage growth in the surface area of the nucleons is recorded by:
solution:
Surface area $x \propto A^{2/3}$
$X_i = 8^{2/3} K = 4K$
$X_f = (8 + 10 + 9)^{2/3} K = 9K$
% increase in surface area of nucleus
$x_i = \dfrac{9K - 4K}{4K} \times 100 = 125\%$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider the following radioactive decay process:

A84218⟶αA1⟶β-A2⟶γA3⟶αA4⟶β+A5⟶γA6

The mass number and the atomic number of A6 are given by:
question:
Consider the following radioactive decay process:

A84218⟶αA1⟶β-A2⟶γA3⟶αA4⟶β+A5⟶γA6

The mass number and the atomic number of A6 are given by:
solution:
When an alpha particle is emitted from nucleus the nucleus loses two proton and two neutrons. This means the atomic mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2.

In beta decays, the mass number of the atoms remains the same, but the atomic number changes: the atomic number increases in the negative beta decay and decreases in the positive beta decay by 1, respectively.

In gamma decay, mass number and atomic number remains unchanged.

Step by step variation is given below:

 84218A⟶α 82214A1⟶β- 83214A2⟶γ 83214A3⟶α 81210A4⟶β+ 80210A5⟶γ 80210A6

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive substances A and B of mass numbers $200$ and $212$ respectively, shows spontaneous $\alpha$-decay with same $Q$ value of $1$ MeV. The ratio of energies of $\alpha$-rays produced by A and B is ________.
question: Two radioactive substances A and B of mass numbers $200$ and $212$ respectively, shows spontaneous $\alpha$-decay with same $Q$ value of $1$ MeV. The ratio of energies of $\alpha$-rays produced by A and B is ________.
solution:
In an $\alpha$-decay, the kinetic energy of the emitted $\alpha$-particle is given by the conservation of momentum and energy as:

$K_{\alpha} = \dfrac{A - 4}{A} Q$

where $A$ is the mass number of the parent nucleus.

For substance A ($A = 200$):

$K_{\alpha, A} = \dfrac{200 - 4}{200} Q = \dfrac{196}{200} Q$

For substance B ($A = 212$):

$K_{\alpha, B} = \dfrac{212 - 4}{212} Q = \dfrac{208}{212} Q$

The ratio of the energies of the $\alpha$-rays produced by A and B is:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{196}{200} Q}{\dfrac{208}{212} Q} = \dfrac{196}{200} \times \dfrac{212}{208}$

Simplifying the fractions:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{49}{50} \times \dfrac{53}{52} = \dfrac{2597}{2600}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2597}{2600}$
